agent_raw:
The retrospective validation does not justify this deployment. The central problem is that training labels — final authorization outcomes — encode payer policy as it existed at each point in time during the study period. Oncology policy is not static: guidelines evolved, biosimilars became available, and billing pathways shifted between inpatient and outpatient during the study window. A model trained to reproduce historical authorization decisions will learn patterns that reflect past policy, not a stable target for future medical appropriateness. As guidelines continue to evolve post-deployment, the model's outputs will increasingly lag the current standard of care.

The evaluation's failure to break out performance by cancer type, therapy line, or policy vintage compounds this problem. A model might agree well with historical approvals for common, guideline-stable regimens while systematically failing on newer immunotherapy regimens or later-line therapies — precisely the cases where authorization decisions are most contested and consequential. Aggregate agreement metrics hide this variation entirely.

Urgent cases routed through expedited physician-to-physician review are systematically underrepresented in the structured training data. These cases involve time-sensitive clinical situations where authorization delays are most dangerous. The model has not been validated on the case population where errors matter most.

Finally, the deployment bundles automated approvals with faster automated denials. In oncology, a wrongly denied authorization can delay chemotherapy, radiation, or targeted therapy in ways that are not reversible. This asymmetric harm is not reflected in the aggregate agreement metrics the vendor presents.

The committee should require subgroup performance analysis by cancer type, therapy line, and policy vintage, and should restrict any initial deployment to auto-approvals for a pre-specified narrow set of stable regimens under physician audit.
all_issues_raised: Training labels encode shifting payer policy rather than stable medical appropriateness
Aggregate agreement metrics hide performance variation by cancer type and therapy line
Urgent expedited-review cases are underrepresented in structured training data
Automated denials carry asymmetric treatment-delay harm not captured by agreement metrics
